---
title: "Supplementary Materials: Principled Default Priors for Bayesian Hypothesis Testing in Binomial GLMs"
shorttitle: "Supplement — Matched Priors for BF Testing in Binomial GLMs"
author:
  - name: Gidon T. Frischkorn
    corresponding: true
    email: gidon.frischkorn@psychologie.uzh.ch
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Zurich
      - name: University of Lucerne
  - name: Joscha Dutli
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Zurich
  - name: Philipp Musfeld
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Amsterdam
      - name: University of Zurich
  - name: Klaus Oberauer
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Zurich
floatsintext: true
format:
  apaquarto-docx: default
  apaquarto-html: default
  apaquarto-pdf:
    documentmode: man
bibliography:
  - references.bib
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggridges)
library(viridis)
library(scales)
library(here)
library(knitr)

options(knitr.table.format = function() if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "latex" else "pandoc")

source(here("R", "link_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prior_sampling.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting.R"))

# Goal 1: Prior predictive results
summaries_pp  <- readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_summaries.rds"))
draws_long    <- readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_draws.rds"))

# RE prior predictive results
re_pp_summaries <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_re_summaries.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)
re_pp_draws <- tryCatch(
  readRDS(here("output", "prior_predictive_re_draws.rds")),
  error = function(e) NULL
)
re_pp_available <- !is.null(re_pp_summaries) && !is.null(re_pp_draws)

# Goal 2: BF calibration results
summaries_bf  <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_summaries.rds"))
continuous_bf <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_continuous.rds"))

# Goal 2 extended: JZS comparison
jzs_available <- file.exists(here("output", "bf_calibration_jzs_summaries.rds"))
if (jzs_available) {
  jzs_summaries <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_jzs_summaries.rds"))
}
logbf_fig_available <- file.exists(here("figures", "logbf_distributions.png"))

# Goal 2 extended: Information / Lindley consistency simulation
consistency_available <- file.exists(here("output", "bf_calibration_consistency_summaries.rds"))
if (consistency_available) {
  consistency_summaries <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_consistency_summaries.rds"))
}

```

## Full Simulation Design

### Prior Predictive Simulation (Step 2)

The prior predictive simulation crossed the following factors:

- **Link function**: logit, probit (2 levels)
- **Intercept prior family** ($b_0$): Normal, Logistic, Cauchy (3 levels)
- **Intercept prior SD** ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_0}$): 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50 (4 levels)
- **Effect prior family** ($b_1$): Normal, Logistic, Cauchy (3 levels)
- **Effect prior SD** ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}$): 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 2.00 (10 levels)

This yields $2 \times 3 \times 4 \times 3 \times 10 = 720$ conditions. For each condition, we drew 10 replications of 10,000 samples using the SimDesign package.

### Bayes Factor Calibration (Step 3)

The BF calibration crossed:

- **Intercept prior family**: Logistic, Normal (2 levels; both at $\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 0.75$)
- **Effect prior family**: Normal, Logistic, Cauchy (3 levels)
- **Effect prior SD**: 0.25, 0.50 (2 levels)
- **True effect** ($b_1$): 0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50 (5 levels)
- **Number of subjects**: 30, 60, 100 (3 levels)
- **Trials per subject**: 20, 50 (2 levels)

This yields $2 \times 3 \times 2 \times 5 \times 3 \times 2 = 360$ conditions with 100 replications each.

A separate random-effects simulation held fixed-effects priors at the recommended matched configuration and varied only the hyperprior: Student-*t*(3, 0, 2.5), Gamma(2, 4), and Exponential(4), yielding 90 conditions with 100 replications each.

### Consistency Simulation

To verify the consistency behaviour of matched-prior Bayes Factors empirically, we ran a focused simulation with the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) prior. We generated data under a between-subjects design: $N = 2 \times n_{\mathrm{subjects}}$ participants each contributing one binomial count from $n_{\mathrm{trials}}$ trials in a single condition ($x \in \{+1, -1\}$, sum-to-zero coded). We included participant-level random intercepts in both data generation and model fitting. The consistency properties examined here depend on the prior density at zero relative to posterior concentration around the true effect; they do not depend on the between- versus within-subjects structure of the design, and the results generalise to within-subjects (repeated-measures) hierarchical models.

- **Intercept prior family**: Logistic (matched; $\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 0.75$)
- **Effect prior family**: Logistic (matched; $\mathrm{sd}_{b_1} = 0.25$)
- **True effect** ($b_1$): 0.00, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50 (6 levels)
- **Number of subjects**: 20, 30, 50, 100, 200 (5 levels; $n_{\mathrm{subjects}}$ denotes participants per condition)
- **Trials per subject**: 20 (fixed)

This yields $6 \times 5 = 30$ conditions with 50 replications each.

@fig-S-consistency shows the median $\log_{10}(\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ as a function of the true effect and the number of subjects. Three properties are visible, and we label them separately. *Monotone evidence growth in effect size*: at any fixed sample size, evidence grows monotonically with the true effect size, so that larger effects produce larger Bayes Factors. (Note that this is weaker than strict information consistency in the sense of @bayarri2012, which requires the BF to diverge for extreme data at fixed $n$; no proper prior attains that property in binomial models, because the likelihood is bounded for finite data.) *Sample-size (Lindley) consistency*: for any fixed non-null effect, evidence for $H_1$ grows with the number of subjects. *Evidence accumulation under the null*: under the true null ($b_1 = 0$), the Bayes Factor increasingly favours $H_0$ as the sample grows. The matched Logistic(0, 0.25) prior thus accumulates evidence in the correct direction along both axes.

```{r}
#| label: fig-S-consistency
#| fig-cap: "Empirical consistency behaviour of the matched Logistic(0, 0.25)
#|   effect prior. Points show the median
#|   $\\log_{10}(\\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ across 50 replications; ribbons (left panel)
#|   span the 25th--75th percentiles. Left: evidence as a function of true effect
#|   size, with one line per sample size (evidence grows monotonically with effect
#|   size at fixed $n$). Right: evidence as a function of sample size, with one
#|   line per true effect (sample-size [Lindley] consistency: evidence for $H_1$
#|   grows with $n$ for non-null effects; separately, under the true null the BF
#|   increasingly favours $H_0$ as $n$ grows). Dashed lines
#|   mark $\\mathrm{BF}_{10} = 3$, $1$, and $1/3$."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-width: 10
#| eval: !expr consistency_available

library(patchwork)

cons <- consistency_summaries |>
  mutate(
    n_lab  = factor(n_subjects, levels = sort(unique(n_subjects))),
    b1_lab = factor(true_b1,    levels = sort(unique(true_b1)))
  )

hlines <- geom_hline(yintercept = c(log10(3), 0, log10(1/3)),
                     linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.4)

p_info <- cons |>
  ggplot(aes(x = true_b1, y = median_log10_BF10,
             colour = n_lab, fill = n_lab, group = n_lab)) +
  hlines +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = q25_log10_BF10, ymax = q75_log10_BF10),
              alpha = 0.12, colour = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d("N subjects", option = "D", end = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d("N subjects", option = "D", end = 0.9) +
  labs(x = expression("True effect " * b[1]),
       y = expression(log[10](BF[10])),
       subtitle = "Evidence growth with effect size") +
  prior_theme()

p_lindley <- cons |>
  filter(true_b1 %in% c(0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n_subjects, y = median_log10_BF10,
             colour = b1_lab, group = b1_lab)) +
  hlines +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sort(unique(cons$n_subjects))) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(expression(b[1]), option = "C", end = 0.9) +
  labs(x = "N subjects (per condition)",
       y = NULL,
       subtitle = "Evidence growth with sample size") +
  prior_theme()

p_info + p_lindley
```

### JZS/Cauchy Comparison Simulation

To directly compare the matched prior against the JZS/Cauchy default [@rouder2012], we crossed prior scale and design factors, fitting both effect-prior families to every simulated dataset:

- **Effect prior scale** ($\mathrm{sd}_{b_1}$): 0.25, $\sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707$ (2 levels)
- **Effect prior family**: Logistic, Cauchy (both fitted to each dataset)
- **True effect** ($b_1$): 0.00, 0.10, 0.20 (3 levels)
- **Number of subjects**: 20, 30, 50 (3 levels)
- **Trials per subject**: 20, 50 (2 levels)

This yields $2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 2 = 36$ conditions with 300 replications each; within every replication, both effect-prior families were fitted to the same dataset, so family comparisons are within-dataset.

### Density Formulas for Analytical Overlays

For the logit link with a $\mathrm{Logistic}(0, s)$ prior on $b_0$: $f(p) = f_{\mathrm{Logis}}(\mathrm{logit}(p), 0, s) / [p(1-p)]$. At $s = 1$, the logistic density and the Jacobian $p(1-p)$ cancel, giving $f(p) = 1$. For the probit link with a $\mathrm{Normal}(0, \sigma)$ prior: $f(p) = \phi(\mathrm{probit}(p)/\sigma) / [\sigma \cdot \phi(\mathrm{probit}(p))]$, where $\phi$ denotes the standard normal density. At $\sigma = 1$, the normal densities cancel, again yielding $f(p) = 1$.


## Comparison to G-Priors {#sec-gprior-comparison}

G-priors [@liang2008] specify that the regression coefficients follow a multivariate normal distribution whose covariance scales with the Fisher information matrix:

$$\beta \sim \mathrm{Normal}\!\left(\beta_0,\; g \cdot I(\beta_0)^{-1}\right)$$

where $g > 0$ is a scalar hyperparameter (or mixture weight) and $I(\beta_0)$ is the Fisher information matrix evaluated at a reference point $\beta_0$. For Gaussian linear models, $I(\beta_0)^{-1} = \sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1}$, giving a prior covariance that is proportional to the ordinary least-squares covariance matrix. @bove2011 and @liclyde2018 extend this framework to GLMs by replacing $(X'X)$ with the GLM Fisher information, which for binomial models is the IRLS weight matrix:

$$I(\beta_0) = X' W(\beta_0) X$$

where $W(\beta_0) = \mathrm{diag}\!\left(p_i(\beta_0)\,[1 - p_i(\beta_0)]\right)$ and $p_i(\beta_0) = g^{-1}(x_i' \beta_0)$ are the predicted probabilities under the reference point $\beta_0$. The g-prior covariance therefore depends on the design matrix $X$ and the weight matrix $W$, evaluated at predicted probabilities that vary with $\beta_0$, $X$, and the link function. Two consequences follow:

1. **The prior scale is design-dependent.** For two datasets with the same number of subjects but different predictor values or designs, the g-prior implies different prior scales on the coefficient scale, and therefore different implied distributions on the probability scale. No universal recommendation like "Logistic(0, 0.75)" is possible.

2. **The prior scale on the probability scale is not analytically tractable.** Because $W(\beta_0)$ depends on the predicted probabilities, the implied distribution of $g^{-1}(\beta)$ on the probability scale cannot be derived analytically. Prior predictive checking must be done numerically for each specific dataset, limiting the use of g-priors as a general diagnostic tool for prior plausibility.

To illustrate the design dependence, consider a simple one-predictor binomial GLM with a single covariate $x$ (sum-to-zero coded, $\pm 1$). For $n$ participants split equally between two conditions:

$$I_{b_1}(\beta_0) = n \cdot p_0(1-p_0)$$

where $p_0 = g^{-1}(b_0)$ is the baseline probability. The g-prior for $b_1$ is then $b_1 \sim \mathrm{Normal}(0,\, [g \cdot n \cdot p_0(1-p_0)]^{-1})$. This implied scale on $b_1$ changes with every dataset: it decreases as $n$ grows (requiring ever-tighter priors as we collect more data), and varies with the baseline probability $p_0$ (being tightest near $p_0 = 0.5$ and becoming increasingly diffuse as $p_0$ approaches 0 or 1). In contrast, the matched prior Logistic(0, 0.75) has a fixed scale that does not depend on $n$, $p_0$, or the design.

The matched prior's design-independence makes it straightforward to reason about and communicate: a researcher can state "I used Logistic(0, 0.25)" and a reader immediately knows the implied prior predictive distribution on the probability scale, regardless of the specific dataset. G-prior scale recommendations, by contrast, are necessarily conditional on the dataset structure.

In practice, g-priors are not readily available in `brms` or Stan, which limits their uptake in applied behavioral research.

### Design-Dependent Scales: A Numerical Illustration

@tbl-gprior-scales illustrates the design dependence quantitatively. For the simple two-group design described above, a fixed-$g$ prior with $g = 1$ implies an effect prior SD of $\mathrm{SD}_g = 1 / \sqrt{n_{\mathrm{total}} \cdot p_0(1-p_0)}$. The *unit-information* choice corresponds to $g = n_{\mathrm{total}}$ rather than $g = 1$; it removes the dependence on sample size — the implied SD becomes $1/\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)}$ — but retains the dependence on the baseline probability. @tbl-gprior-scales shows the implied SD for $g = 1$ across a range of sample sizes and baseline probabilities, alongside the matched prior's fixed SD of 0.25.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-gprior-scales
#| tbl-cap: "Implied SD of a fixed-$g$ g-prior ($g = 1$) for a balanced
#|   two-group binomial GLM across combinations of sample size and baseline
#|   probability. The matched Logistic(0, 0.25) prior always has SD = 0.25,
#|   regardless of design. The g-prior SD varies substantially across these
#|   scenarios: it shrinks as $n$ grows and is smallest near $p_0 = 0.50$.
#|   The shrinkage with $n$ is specific to fixed $g$; the dependence on the
#|   baseline probability $p_0(1-p_0)$ persists for any choice of $g$,
#|   including the unit-information choice $g = n$."

gprior_table <- expand.grid(
  n_subjects = c(30, 60, 100),
  p0         = c(0.30, 0.50, 0.70)
) |>
  mutate(
    n_total     = 2 * n_subjects,
    sd_gprior   = round(1 / sqrt(n_total * p0 * (1 - p0)), 3),
    sd_matched  = 0.25
  ) |>
  select(n_subjects, p0, sd_gprior, sd_matched)

knitr::kable(
  gprior_table,
  col.names = c(
    "$n_{\\text{subjects}}$",
    "$p_0$",
    "G-prior SD ($g = 1$)",
    "Matched Logistic SD"
  ),
  align  = c("r", "r", "r", "r"),
  escape = FALSE
)
```

The fixed-$g$ prior SD ranges from `r min(gprior_table$sd_gprior)` to `r max(gprior_table$sd_gprior)` across these nine scenarios, spanning a threefold range. The g-prior is narrower than the matched prior (SD = 0.25) in larger-sample, central-probability conditions, but wider in smaller-sample or extreme-baseline ones. Because there is no universal relationship between the g-prior scale and the matched prior scale, no single g-prior recommendation can substitute for the matched-prior approach.

### Prior Predictive Implications of G-Priors

@fig-S3-gprior-pp shows the prior predictive distributions on the effect scale ($\delta p$) for the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) prior and the fixed-$g$ prior ($g = 1$) across three representative design scenarios. For the matched prior, the distribution is identical across scenarios by design: a researcher applying Logistic(0, 0.25) obtains the same prior predictive guarantees regardless of sample size or baseline. For the fixed-$g$ prior, the distribution shifts across scenarios: the prior concentrates more tightly on small effects in larger samples and spreads more broadly at extreme baselines.

```{r}
#| label: fig-S3-gprior-pp
#| fig-cap: "Prior predictive distributions of $\\delta p$ for the matched
#|   Logistic(0, 0.25) prior (dark) and a fixed-$g$ prior ($g = 1$,
#|   light) across three design scenarios. The matched prior's distribution is
#|   identical in all three panels by construction. The g-prior distribution
#|   shifts across scenarios: it concentrates more tightly at larger $n$
#|   ($n = 100$, centre panel) and spreads more broadly at an extreme baseline
#|   probability ($p_0 = 0.30$, right panel). Dotted lines mark
#|   $|\\delta p| = 0.10$ and $|\\delta p| = 0.20$."
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 11

set.seed(2024)
n_gpp <- 30000

# Design scenarios: n_subjects, p0 → g-prior SD
gprior_scen <- data.frame(
  n_subjects = c(30, 100, 30),
  p0         = c(0.50, 0.50, 0.30),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
gprior_scen$n_total   <- 2 * gprior_scen$n_subjects
gprior_scen$sd_gprior <- 1 / sqrt(gprior_scen$n_total *
                                     gprior_scen$p0 * (1 - gprior_scen$p0))
gprior_scen$label <- paste0(
  "n = ", gprior_scen$n_subjects, ", p0 = ", gprior_scen$p0,
  "  (SD[g] = ", round(gprior_scen$sd_gprior, 2), ")"
)

# Matched prior draws (same for all scenarios)
b0_base    <- rlogis(n_gpp, 0, 0.75)
b1_matched <- rlogis(n_gpp, 0, 0.25)
dp_matched <- plogis(b0_base + b1_matched) - plogis(b0_base - b1_matched)

# Build combined data frame
gpp_rows <- lapply(seq_len(nrow(gprior_scen)), function(i) {
  sc <- gprior_scen[i, ]
  b0 <- rlogis(n_gpp, 0, 0.75)
  b1_g <- rnorm(n_gpp, 0, sc$sd_gprior)
  dp_g <- plogis(b0 + b1_g) - plogis(b0 - b1_g)
  rbind(
    data.frame(delta_p = dp_matched, label = sc$label,
               prior = "Matched Logistic(0, 0.25)",
               stringsAsFactors = FALSE),
    data.frame(delta_p = dp_g, label = sc$label,
               prior = paste0("G-prior (g = 1; SD = ",
                              round(sc$sd_gprior, 2), ")"),
               stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  )
})
gpp_data <- do.call(rbind, gpp_rows) |>
  mutate(
    prior = factor(prior, levels = c(
      "Matched Logistic(0, 0.25)",
      paste0("G-prior (g = 1; SD = ",
             round(gprior_scen$sd_gprior, 2), ")")
    ))
  )

ggplot(gpp_data, aes(x = delta_p, colour = prior, fill = prior)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-0.20, -0.10, 0.10, 0.20),
             linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.4, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.5, colour = "grey20") +
  facet_wrap(~ label, nrow = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(expression(delta * italic(p)),
                     limits = c(-1, 1),
                     breaks = seq(-1, 1, 0.25)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d("Prior", option = "D", end = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d("Prior", option = "D", end = 0.85) +
  prior_theme() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 9))
```

### Properties Against Formal Criteria {#sec-bayarri-desiderata}

@bayarri2012 propose a set of criteria for evaluating objective priors used in Bayesian model comparison. The main paper summarises which of these properties matched priors possess (see the Summary of Step 3 in the main text). Here we assess the properties in more detail and compare matched priors to g-priors for GLMs, distinguishing which properties are provable from which are demonstrated empirically.

**Monotone evidence growth in effect size.** At a fixed sample size, the matched-prior Bayes factor increases monotonically with the true effect size, so larger effects yield stronger evidence for $H_1$ (see the Consistency Simulation above). Fixed-$g$ priors, by contrast, suffer from the *information paradox* described by @liang2008 for the Gaussian linear model: because the prior contracts as the information grows, the Bayes factor can remain bounded even as $R^2 \to 1$, a setting in which unbounded evidence would be attainable. Mixture-of-$g$ priors (e.g., the ZS-adapted prior of @liang2008) resolve this paradox, but in the context of GLMs their evaluation requires Laplace approximation [@liclyde2018], which introduces additional approximation error and is not accessible via standard software such as `brms`.

**Model selection consistency.** Highly diffuse priors exhibit Bartlett's paradox: as $g \to \infty$ (i.e., as the prior becomes arbitrarily diffuse), the Bayes factor collapses to favour $H_0$ regardless of the data. A fixed, *finite* $g$ avoids Bartlett's paradox, at the cost of the information paradox above. Matched priors with fixed, finite scale likewise avoid this problem; model selection consistency (the probability of correctly identifying the true model approaching 1 as $n \to \infty$) follows from standard Bayesian consistency results for proper priors with full support at the true parameter value. Mixture-of-$g$ priors also achieve model selection consistency under regularity conditions [@liang2008].

**Invariance to group labelling.** @bayarri2012 discuss *measurement invariance* — invariance to changes in the outcome's measurement units. Binomial GLMs have no unit-bearing outcome, so the closest analogue is invariance to arbitrary group labels. Under sum-to-zero $\pm 1$ contrast coding, the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) prior implies a symmetric prior predictive distribution for $\delta p$: reversing the sign of the predictor reverses the sign of $b_1$ but leaves $|\delta p|$ unchanged. The Bayes factor therefore does not depend on which group is arbitrarily labelled $+1$ or $-1$. G-prior scales are determined by the Fisher information matrix evaluated at a reference point $\beta_0$, which depends on the design structure; the resulting symmetry properties depend on the specific design.

**Interpretable prior predictive on the probability scale.** This property is related to, but weaker than, *predictive matching* in @bayarri2012, which concerns marginal predictive distributions at minimal sample sizes. In our workflow, the matched prior yields an analytically known prior predictive on the probability scale by construction: because the prior family mirrors the inverse link function (probability integral transform), the prior predictive distribution on the probability scale is exactly uniform at scale 1 and plausibly concentrated at scale 0.75. G-prior prior predictive distributions on the probability scale are not analytically tractable (see @tbl-gprior-scales and @fig-S3-gprior-pp) and must be numerically evaluated for each specific dataset, limiting their use as a diagnostic tool.

@tbl-bayarri-comparison summarises the comparison.

| Property | Matched prior | G-prior (fixed $g$) | Mixture-of-$g$ |
|-----------|:-------------|:-------------------|:--------------|
| Monotone evidence growth in effect size (fixed $n$) | Yes (empirical) | No (information paradox) | Yes |
| Model selection consistency ($n \to \infty$) | Yes | Yes for finite $g$ (fails as $g \to \infty$; Bartlett's paradox) | Yes |
| Invariance to group labelling | Yes (sum-to-zero coding) | Design-dependent | Design-dependent |
| Interpretable prior predictive on probability scale | Yes (by construction via Step 2) | Not directly accessible | Not directly accessible |
| Available in `brms`/Stan | Yes | No | No |

: Comparison of matched priors, fixed-$g$ priors, and mixture-of-$g$ priors. Property list adapted from the criteria of @bayarri2012; the first row reports monotone growth of evidence with effect size, the property our simulations demonstrate. References: information paradox and mixture-of-$g$ [@liang2008]; GLM extension [@liclyde2018; @bove2011]. {#tbl-bayarri-comparison}


## Full Bayes Factor Calibration Results {#sec-full-bf-results}

The main paper reports BF calibration results for a representative subset ($n_{\text{subjects}} = 30$, 20 trials, true $b_1 \in \{0, 0.10, 0.20\}$). Here we report the full results for the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) effect prior across all 5 true effect sizes, 3 sample sizes, and 2 trial counts.

### Detection Rates for the Matched Prior

@tbl-bf-detection shows detection rates ($P(\mathrm{BF}_{10} > 3)$) for the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) prior with a matched Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior. Under the null ($b_1 = 0$), the rate of misleading evidence for $H_1$ ($P(\mathrm{BF}_{10} > 3)$ under the null) stays below 5% in all conditions. Detection rates increase with effect size and sample size as expected, and are substantially higher with 50 versus 20 trials per subject.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-bf-detection
#| tbl-cap: "Detection rates ($P(\\mathrm{BF}_{10} > 3)$) for the matched
#|   Logistic(0, 0.25) effect prior across all simulation conditions. Columns
#|   show results for each combination of sample size ($n$) and trials per
#|   subject ($T$). Rows correspond to true effect sizes on the logit scale.
#|   Under the null ($b_1 = 0$), rates reflect misleading evidence for $H_1$.
#|   Each rate is estimated from 100 replications per condition; the Monte Carlo
#|   standard error of an estimated rate $p$ is $\\sqrt{p(1-p)/100} \\leq 0.05$."

rates_table <- summaries_bf |>
  filter(dist_b0 == "logistic", dist_b1 == "logistic", sd_b1 == 0.25) |>
  mutate(
    col_label = paste0("n=", n_subjects, " T=", n_trials)
  ) |>
  select(true_b1, col_label, P_BF10_gt3) |>
  mutate(P_BF10_gt3 = round(P_BF10_gt3, 2)) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = col_label, values_from = P_BF10_gt3)

knitr::kable(
  rates_table,
  col.names  = c("True $b_1$", colnames(rates_table)[-1]),
  align      = c("l", rep("r", ncol(rates_table) - 1)),
  escape     = FALSE
)
```

### BF Distributions Across All Conditions

@fig-S4-bf-full shows the full pattern of Bayes Factor evidence across all conditions as a heatmap of median $\log_{10}(\mathrm{BF}_{10})$. Each cell shows the central tendency of the BF distribution for one simulation condition, with positive values indicating evidence for $H_1$ (an effect exists) and negative values indicating evidence for $H_0$ (no effect). The matched prior yields calibrated evidence: under the null, median BFs are slightly below 1, reflecting appropriate support for $H_0$ with moderate-to-large samples.

```{r}
#| label: fig-S4-bf-full
#| fig-cap: "Median $\\log_{10}(\\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ across all simulation
#|   conditions for the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) effect prior with matched
#|   Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior. Rows correspond to true effect sizes;
#|   columns to sample sizes. The two panels show results for 20 (left) and 50
#|   (right) trials per subject. Positive values (yellow) indicate evidence for
#|   $H_1$; negative values (dark) indicate evidence for $H_0$. Dashed contour
#|   at $\\log_{10}(\\mathrm{BF}) = \\pm \\log_{10}(3) \\approx \\pm 0.48$ marks the
#|   conventional BF = 3 threshold."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10

bf_heatmap_data <- continuous_bf |>
  filter(dist_b0 == "logistic", dist_b1 == "logistic", sd_b1 == 0.25) |>
  mutate(
    n_trials_label  = paste0("Trials per subject: ", n_trials),
    true_b1_label   = factor(paste0("b[1] == ", true_b1),
                             levels = paste0("b[1] == ",
                                             sort(unique(true_b1)))),
    n_subjects_fac  = factor(n_subjects)
  )

ggplot(bf_heatmap_data,
       aes(x = n_subjects_fac, y = true_b1_label, fill = median_log10_BF10)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", median_log10_BF10)),
            size = 3.2, colour = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ n_trials_label, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    name   = expression(median ~ log[10](BF[10])),
    low    = "#440154", mid = "grey90", high = "#FDE725",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits   = c(-1.5, 2.5),
    oob      = scales::squish
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete("Number of subjects") +
  scale_y_discrete("True effect size", labels = scales::label_parse()) +
  prior_theme() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 9))
```


## JZS/Cauchy Comparison: Full Results {#sec-jzs-full-results}

@tbl-S-jzs-rates gives full detection-rate results for the JZS/Cauchy comparison, supplementing the violin-plot figure in the main paper. It shows $P(\mathrm{BF}_{10} > 3)$ (detection rate) and $P(\mathrm{BF}_{01} > 3)$ (false-null support) for both the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) and the JZS Cauchy(0, $\sqrt{2}/2$) prior.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-S-jzs-rates
#| tbl-cap: "Detection rates ($P(\\mathrm{BF}_{10} > 3)$) and false-null support
#|   rates ($P(\\mathrm{BF}_{01} > 3)$) for the matched Logistic(0, 0.25) and
#|   JZS Cauchy(0, $\\sqrt{2}/2$) priors across all simulation conditions.
#|   Under the true null ($b_1 = 0$), $P(\\mathrm{BF}_{01} > 3)$ reflects correct
#|   support for $H_0$; under nonzero effects, it reflects false support for $H_0$.
#|   $n$: number of subjects; $T$: trials per subject. Each rate is estimated
#|   from 300 replications per condition; the Monte Carlo standard error of an
#|   estimated rate $p$ is $\\sqrt{p(1-p)/300} \\leq 0.03$."
#| eval: !expr jzs_available

jzs_tbl <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_jzs_rates_table.rds")) |>
  filter((prior == "logistic" & sd_b1 == 0.25) |
         (prior == "cauchy"   & sd_b1 > 0.7)) |>
  mutate(
    prior_label = if_else(prior == "logistic",
                          "Matched Logistic(0, 0.25)",
                          "JZS Cauchy(0, sqrt(2)/2)"),
    P_BF10_gt3 = round(P_BF10_gt3, 2),
    P_BF01_gt3 = round(P_BF01_gt3, 2),
    col_label  = paste0("n=", n_subjects, " T=", n_trials)
  ) |>
  select(prior_label, true_b1, col_label, P_BF10_gt3, P_BF01_gt3) |>
  pivot_longer(c(P_BF10_gt3, P_BF01_gt3),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "val") |>
  mutate(metric = recode(metric,
                         P_BF10_gt3 = "P(BF10>3)",
                         P_BF01_gt3 = "P(BF01>3)")) |>
  unite("prior_metric", prior_label, metric, sep = " | ") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = col_label, values_from = val) |>
  arrange(prior_metric, true_b1)

knitr::kable(
  jzs_tbl,
  col.names = c("Prior | Metric", "True $b_1$",
                colnames(jzs_tbl)[-(1:2)]),
  align  = c("l", "r", rep("r", ncol(jzs_tbl) - 2)),
  escape = FALSE
)
```

@fig-S-jzs-logbf shows the full distribution of $\log_{10}(\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ for all four prior combinations across the design space, underlying the ROC analysis in the main paper. At matched scales, Logistic and Cauchy produce nearly identical distributions — within each ridge, solid and dashed lines virtually coincide. The scale difference is the dominant factor: under the true null ($b_1 = 0$), scale 0.707 shifts the distribution substantially leftward relative to the $\pm\log_{10}(3)$ decision boundaries, reducing BF mass in the evidence-for-$H_1$ region. Prior scale moves BF mass relative to the decision thresholds; prior family does not.

```{r}
#| label: fig-S-jzs-logbf
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of $\\log_{10}(\\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ for all four prior
#|   combinations (Logistic/Cauchy $\\times$ scale 0.25/0.707) under the null
#|   ($b_1 = 0$), a small effect ($b_1 = 0.10$), and a medium effect ($b_1 = 0.20$).
#|   Columns show sample sizes ($N = 20, 30, 50$); rows show trial counts ($T = 20, 50$).
#|   Within each ridge, solid (Logistic) and dashed (Cauchy) lines nearly coincide
#|   at matched scales. Scale 0.707 shifts both distributions leftward relative to
#|   the $\\pm\\log_{10}(3)$ decision thresholds (vertical dashed lines), reducing
#|   BF mass in the evidence-for-$H_1$ region."
#| out-width: "100%"
#| eval: !expr logbf_fig_available
knitr::include_graphics(here("figures", "logbf_distributions.png"))
```

@fig-S-jzs-heatmap shows the median $\log_{10}(\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ across all JZS comparison conditions as a heatmap, with the two priors in separate columns. This complements the distributional view by showing the full factorial pattern across sample sizes, trial counts, and effect sizes.

```{r}
#| label: fig-S-jzs-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Median $\\log_{10}(\\mathrm{BF}_{10})$ for the matched Logistic(0, 0.25)
#|   (left columns) and JZS Cauchy(0, $\\sqrt{2}/2$) (right columns) priors across
#|   all simulation conditions. Rows: true effect size. Columns: sample size and
#|   prior. Panels: trials per subject. Positive values (yellow) indicate evidence
#|   for $H_1$; negative values (dark) indicate evidence for $H_0$."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 11
#| eval: !expr jzs_available

jzs_continuous <- readRDS(here("output", "bf_calibration_jzs_continuous.rds")) |>
  filter((prior == "logistic" & sd_b1 == 0.25) |
         (prior == "cauchy"   & sd_b1 > 0.7)) |>
  mutate(
    prior_label    = case_when(
      prior == "logistic" ~ "Matched\nLogistic(0, 0.25)",
      prior == "cauchy"   ~ "JZS\nCauchy(0, sqrt(2)/2)"
    ),
    n_trials_label = paste0("Trials per subject: ", n_trials),
    true_b1_label  = factor(paste0("b[1] == ", true_b1),
                            levels = paste0("b[1] == ", sort(unique(true_b1)))),
    n_subjects_fac = factor(n_subjects)
  )

ggplot(jzs_continuous,
       aes(x = interaction(n_subjects_fac, prior_label, sep = "\n"),
           y = true_b1_label,
           fill = median_log10_BF10)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", median_log10_BF10)),
            size = 2.8, colour = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  facet_wrap(~ n_trials_label, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    name     = expression(median ~ log[10](BF[10])),
    low      = "#440154", mid = "grey90", high = "#FDE725",
    midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1.5, 2.5), oob = scales::squish
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete("n subjects / Prior") +
  scale_y_discrete("True effect size", labels = scales::label_parse()) +
  prior_theme() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.key.width = unit(2, "cm"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))
```




## Supplementary Figures

```{r}
#| label: fig-S1-floor-ceiling
#| fig-cap: "Proportion of prior predictive mass in the floor/ceiling region
#|   (p < 0.05 or p > 0.95) as a function of intercept prior SD and family.
#|   The matched prior at $\\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 1.0$ places exactly 10% in this region
#|   (2 x 0.05 = 0.10), confirming the uniform result. At $\\mathrm{sd}_{b_0} = 0.75$,
#|   floor/ceiling mass drops below 10% for both matched priors."
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 10

plot_floor_ceiling_heatmap(summaries_pp)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-S2-re-delta-p
#| fig-cap: "Effect size heterogeneity by hyperprior family and scale for the
#|   intercept + slope model. At matched scale, all families produce similar
#|   effect heterogeneity. Dotted lines mark +/-0.10, +/-0.20, +/-0.30."
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 12
#| eval: !expr re_pp_available

re_pp_draws |>
  filter(re_structure == "Intercept + slope") |>
  group_by(hp_family, hp_mean) |>
  slice_sample(n = 10000) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(hp_mean_label = paste0("mu == ", hp_mean)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = delta_p, y = hp_family,
             fill = hp_family, colour = hp_family)) +
  geom_density_ridges(stat = "binline", bins = 80,
                      alpha = 0.5, scale = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.5, colour = "grey20") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-0.30, -0.20, -0.10, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30),
             linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.5, colour = "grey40") +
  facet_wrap(~ hp_mean_label, ncol = 2,
             labeller = label_parsed) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D", end = 0.85, guide = "none") +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "D", end = 0.85, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expression(delta * italic(p)),
    limits = c(-1, 1), breaks = seq(-1, 1, 0.25)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete("Family") +
  prior_theme()
```
